summary: Overall market sentiment is decidedly risk-off, with the S&P 500 and Dow ticking lower as escalating U.S.-Iran military confrontation in the Strait of Hormuz rattles investor confidence and fuels recession fears. The dominant macro driver is the intensifying Persian Gulf crisis, marked by Iran blocking U.S. warships from the Strait of Hormuz, a fire and explosion hitting a South Korean-operated vessel, and shipping at a near standstill, pushing energy supply disruption fears to the forefront while Treasury Secretary Bessent urges China to apply diplomatic pressure on Iran ahead of a Trump-Xi summit. On the sector and stock level, surging gasoline prices are hitting consumer discretionary names hard — evidenced by declining U.S. restaurant sales — while the dollar is strengthening, European equities and bonds are selling off, gold is slipping as the strong dollar offsets safe-haven demand, and AI infrastructure remains a rare bright spot with Bret Taylor's Sierra raising nearly $1 billion in fresh venture capital. The principal risks investors are monitoring include a prolonged Hormuz closure triggering a sustained oil price shock, China's invocation of its anti-sanctions law to counter U.S. blacklisting of refiners deepening geopolitical trade tensions, and analysts warning of "misplaced euphoria" in markets that may be underpricing a recession scenario. In the short term, volatility is likely to remain elevated, with market direction heavily contingent on diplomatic developments between Washington, Tehran, and Beijing, and any further military escalation in the Gulf capable of delivering a sharp downside shock to equities and consumer spending.
